trajectory_summary: The call is procedurally complete — verify items collected, bill captured, appointment booked — but the energy Kim brought at 00:58 was never converted into appointment anticipation. Bucky ran a checklist, not a conversation, and the booking carries elevated no-show risk because Kim has no emotional reason to show up beyond a calendar entry.
outcome_dispute: The call produced a booked appointment, so a 'booked' tag is defensible. However, the appointment is structurally soft: husband on title was not included in the booking, button-up protocol was largely skipped (no time anchor, no computer requirement, no 'is that fair' handshake, no text follow-up promise), and Kim's engagement peaked at 00:58 and was never re-engaged. A 'booked' tag without a fragility flag understates the no-show risk.

primary_coaching_focus.topic: Button-up protocol — all five elements missing
primary_coaching_focus.quote: K. I'll get working on your design, and then we'll see you on Monday.
primary_coaching_focus.why: Bucky booked the appointment but skipped every element that predicts whether Kim actually shows up: no one-hour block, no phone-appointment specification, no computer/not-driving requirement, no 'is that fair' handshake, no text follow-up promise. Combined with the husband not being included, this appointment has elevated no-show risk. The manager should walk Bucky through the button_up sequence element by element against this call, then role-play it until the sequence is automatic.

key_moments.quote: Yeah. We wanna get a pool heater, but we wanted to, run it off solar.
key_moments.stewart_read: This is the call's highest-value moment. Kim volunteers a specific, load-increasing use case — a pool heater running off solar — which signals both genuine motivation and a higher-than-average system size need. Bucky writes it down and moves on. No emotional amplification, no curiosity creation, no tie-back to what the specialist will show her. The strategic read would be that Bucky is saving the amplification for the button-up — but there is no evidence of that; the pool heater is never mentioned again.

key_moments.quote: And do you guys have, like, like, generators for the solar panels?
key_moments.stewart_read: Kim's follow-up question about generators/batteries is a second consecutive enthusiasm signal — she is already thinking about backup power, which is a Powerwall conversation. Bucky's response ('We have batteries that we might have generators. You can talk to the so — I'm basically, I'm just building design, so you talk to the specialist') correctly deflects to the specialist but the hedge ('we might have generators') introduces uncertainty where there should be confidence. Ion carries the Tesla Powerwall as its battery product; the answer is 'yes, we have battery backup' — the generator hedge is unnecessary and slightly undermines legitimacy.

key_moments.quote: Okay. This is Lucky with Ion. Okay?
key_moments.stewart_read: Bucky introduces himself as 'Lucky' in the text follow-up moment, having introduced himself as 'Bucky' at 00:04. This is a name inconsistency that a suspicious or detail-oriented customer would notice. Kim does not flag it, but it is a small legitimacy crack in a category (solar) where scam anxiety is already elevated. It may be a transcription artifact, but if it reflects what Kim actually received in the text, it is a trust-eroding detail.

key_moments.quote: Is there any else is there anyone else on the pedal but you?
key_moments.stewart_read: Bucky asks about co-owners and Kim confirms her husband is on the title at 00:44. Bucky acknowledges and moves on — the husband is never mentioned again, and the appointment is booked for Kim alone. This is a protocol violation: per codex, a spouse on title must be included in the appointment. Bucky captured the information and then did not act on it.
